title: SEGMENT 10 — iPSC quality control: what makes a good iPSC

lines.text: SEGMENT 10 — iPSC quality control: what makes a good iPSC
We're back.
Hope you stretched your legs.
Let's pick up with a question that sounds boring but is actually critical for everything that follows: how do you know if an iPSC line is any good?
This sounds like a stupid question — surely you can just look at the cells and tell?
But it turns out reprogrammed cells exist on a spectrum.
Some are fully pluripotent, indistinguishable from ES cells.
Some are partially reprogrammed, expressing some pluripotency markers but not others, with chromatin states halfway between fibroblast and ES.
Some have acquired random mutations during the reprogramming process — chromosomal abnormalities, copy number changes, point mutations.
Some carry residual epigenetic memory of the cell they came from, which biases what they like to differentiate into.
A real iPSC line, before you use it for anything, has to pass a suite of quality control tests.
Here are the main ones.
Test one: morphology.
Under the microscope, true iPSC colonies look very distinctive.
They're tight, dome-shaped, with crisp edges.
Individual cells inside the colony are small with a high nucleus-to-cytoplasm ratio.
If the colonies look ragged, or if cells are spilling out and flattening, that's a sign the line isn't fully pluripotent or has started differentiating.
Experienced iPSC technicians can tell, at a glance, whether a colony looks right.
Test two: pluripotency marker expression.
You stain the cells for proteins that should be on in pluripotent cells — Oct4 (the endogenous one, not the transgene), Sox2, Nanog, SSEA-4, TRA-1-60.
Bona fide iPSCs express all of these robustly.
Lines that have lost expression of one or more pluripotency markers are suspect.
Test three: differentiation potential — the embryoid body assay.
You let the iPSCs aggregate in suspension culture without the factors that maintain pluripotency.
They spontaneously form little balls called embryoid bodies, and within those balls they start differentiating into all three germ layers.
You then stain the embryoid bodies for ectoderm markers, mesoderm markers, endoderm markers.
A real iPSC line gives you all three.
A line that's restricted — that only differentiates well into, say, ectoderm — is partially functional but not fully pluripotent.
Test four: the teratoma assay.
This is the gold standard, and it's a little gruesome.
You take a clump of iPSCs and inject them under the skin or into the testis of an immunocompromised mouse.
Over the next six to eight weeks, the cells form a benign tumor — a teratoma — that grows into a chaotic mass containing tissues from all three germ layers.
You section the tumor, stain it, and look for the diagnostic features: glandular structures (endoderm), muscle and cartilage (mesoderm), neural rosettes and pigmented retinal epithelium (ectoderm).
If the teratoma contains all three germ layers represented by recognizable tissue, the cells were pluripotent.
The analogy is: the teratoma is the smoking gun for pluripotency — inject iPSCs into a mouse, and a benign tumor forms with bone, gut, hair, neurons, all three germ layers.
It's a chaotic mini-embryo that grew in the wrong place.
Disturbing, but diagnostic.
Test five: transcriptome comparison.
You do RNA sequencing on the iPSCs and on reference ES cells.
The two transcriptomes should be very similar — most genes expressed at similar levels, similar pluripotency signature.
If the iPSC transcriptome shows hallmarks of the original fibroblast state lingering — fibroblast genes still on, ES genes incompletely activated — that's incomplete reprogramming.
Test six: karyotype.
You do a chromosome spread and check that the iPSC has the normal complement of 46 chromosomes, in the right configuration.
Reprogramming, and prolonged culture afterward, can introduce chromosomal abnormalities — trisomies, large deletions, translocations.
Lines with abnormal karyotypes are not safe to use for downstream applications, especially clinical ones.
Test seven: integration-free verification.
If you used Sendai virus or episomal plasmids, you check by PCR that the iPSC has actually lost the reprogramming vector.
If the Sendai genome is still detectable, you let the cells grow for more passages until it's gone.
Test eight: genomic integrity at higher resolution.
Modern iPSC lines, especially those intended for clinical use, get whole-genome sequenced to look for point mutations and copy number variations introduced during reprogramming.
Some mutations are tolerable; mutations in cancer driver genes are not.
A good iPSC line, by 2026 standards, has passed all of these tests.

It has normal morphology, expresses all pluripotency markers, differentiates into all three germ layers in embryoid bodies, forms a clean three-germ-layer teratoma, has a transcriptome very close to ES cells, has a normal karyotype, has no detectable reprogramming vector, and has no mutations in cancer genes.
Making such a line takes months and costs tens of thousands of dollars.
For research use, you can be a little looser.
For clinical use — for cells you're about to put into a patient — every test has to be done, with documentation, under regulatory standards.
The bar is appropriately high.

The reason the bar matters: if you skip QC and just use whatever you get, you can end up with cells that behave strangely, differentiate poorly, contain dangerous mutations, or worst of all, contain a small fraction of incompletely reprogrammed cells that will form tumors when transplanted.
The history of the field has several embarrassing episodes where promising clinical programs were derailed by inadequate QC on the starting cells.
Don't be that lab.
